subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A uniform rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and Length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> at rest on a smooth horizontal surface. An impulse of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is applied to end B. The time taken by the rod to turn through at right angles will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ____.
question: A uniform rod <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and Length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> at rest on a smooth horizontal surface. An impulse of force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is applied to end B. The time taken by the rod to turn through at right angles will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> ____.
solution:
Impulse J=0.2 N s

⇒J=∫Fdt=0.2 N s

Now, angular impulse (M→) will be

Mc=∫τdt

=∫FL2dt

=L2∫Fdt=L2×J

=0.32×0.2

=0.03

Moment of inertia of the rod about the centre of mass, Icm=ML212=2×(0.3)212=0.096

Angular impulse will be equal to the change in angular momentum.

M=Icmωf-ωi

⇒0.03=0.096ωf

⇒ωf=2 rad s-1

For angular displacement, we can write θ=ωt

⇒t=θω=π2×2=π4 s.

Therefore, x=4.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
In two different experiments, an object of mass 5 kg moving with a speed of 25 ms-1 hits two different walls and comes to rest within
(i) 3 second, (ii) 5 seconds, respectively.
Choose the correct option out of the following :
question:
In two different experiments, an object of mass 5 kg moving with a speed of 25 ms-1 hits two different walls and comes to rest within
(i) 3 second, (ii) 5 seconds, respectively.
Choose the correct option out of the following :
options: Impulse and average force acting on the object will be same for both the cases.
Impulse will be same for both the cases but the average force will be different.
Average force will be same for both the cases but the impulse will be different.
Average force and impulse will be different for both the cases.
solution:
Impulse = Change in momentum

I=ΔP

Therefore, in both the cases the impulse is same.

Average force is defined as,

Favg =ΔPΔt

Since the time in the two cases is different, the average force will be different.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Body P having mass M moving with speed u has head-on collision elastically with another body Q having mass m initially at rest. If m≪M, body Q will have a maximum speed equal to 2u after collision.

Reason R : During elastic collision, the momentum and kinetic energy are both conserved.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.

Assertion A : Body P having mass M moving with speed u has head-on collision elastically with another body Q having mass m initially at rest. If m≪M, body Q will have a maximum speed equal to 2u after collision.

Reason R : During elastic collision, the momentum and kinetic energy are both conserved.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: A is correct but R is not correct.
A is not correct but R is correct.
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
solution:
For e=1 & second body at rest

V2=2m1u1 m1+m2=2uMM+m≃2u

Since M>>m

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A man (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) and his son (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) are standing on a frictionless surface facing each other. The man pushes his son so that he starts moving at a speed of <math><mn>0.70</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> with respect to the man. The speed of the man with respect to the surface is:
question: A man (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>50</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) and his son (mass <math><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) are standing on a frictionless surface facing each other. The man pushes his son so that he starts moving at a speed of <math><mn>0.70</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> with respect to the man. The speed of the man with respect to the surface is:
solution:
By conservation of linear momentum,

P→i=P→f

Where P→i and P→f are initial and final momentum of system (boy + man)

Now,  0=mbv→b+mmv→m......(i)

vb= velocity of boy w.r.t. ground

vm= velocity of man w.r.t. ground

v→bm= velocity of boy w.r.t. man

So, v→bm=v→b-v→m

or v→b=v→bm+v→m

or v→b=0.7+v→m ........(ii)

From equation (i) and (ii),

0=200.7+v→m+50 v→m

v→m=-1470=-0.20 m s-1

Negative sign shows velocity of man is opposite to boy.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A proton of mass $\mathrm{m}$ collides elastically with a particle of unknown mass at rest. After the collision, the proton and the unknown particle are seen moving at an angle of $90^{\circ}$ with respect to each other. The mass of unknown particle is:
question: A proton of mass $\mathrm{m}$ collides elastically with a particle of unknown mass at rest. After the collision, the proton and the unknown particle are seen moving at an angle of $90^{\circ}$ with respect to each other. The mass of unknown particle is:
solution:
Apply principle of conservation of momentum along $x$-direction, $$ \begin{aligned} m u &=m v_1 \cos 45^{\circ}+M v_2 \cos 45^{\circ} \\ m u &=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(m v_1+M v_2\right) \end{aligned} $$ Along $y$-direction, $$ \begin{aligned} &o=m v_1 \sin 45^{\circ}-M v_2 \sin 45^{\circ} \\ &o=\left(m v_1-M v_2\right) \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned} $$
Coefficient of restution $e=1$ $$ =\frac{v_2-v_1 \cos 90}{u \cos 45} $$ ( $\because$ Collision is elastic) $$ \begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow \frac{v_2}{\frac{u}{\sqrt{2}}}=1 \\ &\Rightarrow u=\sqrt{2} v_2 \end{aligned} $$ Solving eqs (i), (ii), \& (iii), we get mass of unknown particle, $M=m$

q: The mass of a hydrogen molecule is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.32</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27 </mn></mrow></msup><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup></math> hydrogen molecules strike, per second, a fixed wall of the area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> at an angle of <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> to the normal, and rebound elastically with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the pressure on the wall is nearly:
question: The mass of a hydrogen molecule is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.32</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27 </mn></mrow></msup><mi>kg</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup></math> hydrogen molecules strike, per second, a fixed wall of the area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> at an angle of <math><msup><mrow><mn>45</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup></math> to the normal, and rebound elastically with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> then the pressure on the wall is nearly:
solution:
F=2mnVcosθ⇒P=2mnVcosθA

⇒P=2×3.32×10-27×1023×1032×2×10-4

⇒P=2.35×103 N m-2

q: A circular hole of radius a2 is cut out of a circular disc of radius a as shown in figure. The centroid of the remaining circular portion with respect to point O will be:
question: A circular hole of radius a2 is cut out of a circular disc of radius a as shown in figure. The centroid of the remaining circular portion with respect to point O will be:

xCOM=σπa2a-σπa24×3a2σπa2-σπa24

=a-3a81-14=5a6

q: A uniform rectangular thin sheet ABCD of mass M has length a and breadth b, as shown in the figure. If the shaded portion HBGO is cut-off, the coordinates of the centre of mass of the remaining portion will be:
question: A uniform rectangular thin sheet ABCD of mass M has length a and breadth b, as shown in the figure. If the shaded portion HBGO is cut-off, the coordinates of the centre of mass of the remaining portion will be:
solution:
If the total mass of object mass M then the mass removed part will be M4 .

XCM=M·a2-M43a4M-M4=Ma2-3Ma163M4=512a
YCM=M·b2-M43b4M-M4=Mb2-3Mb163M4=512b

q: The position vector of the center of mass r→cm  of an asymmetric uniform bar of negligible area of cross-section as shown in figure is:
question: The position vector of the center of mass r→cm  of an asymmetric uniform bar of negligible area of cross-section as shown in figure is:
solution:
The position vector of center of mass r→cm is given as 

r→cm=Xcmx^+Ycm y^

Where, Xcm=x-coordinate of center of mass  and Ycm=y-coordinate of center of mass

r→cm=m1x1+m2x2+m3x3m1+m2+m3 x^ +m1y1+m2y2+m3y3m1+m2+m3 y^

r→cm=2m×L+m×2L+m5L22m+m+mx^+2m×L+m×L2+m×02m+m+my^

r→cm=13L8x^+5L8y^

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> makes an elastic collision with a second body at rest and continues to move in the original direction but with one fourth of its original speed. What is the mass of the second body?
question: A body of mass <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> makes an elastic collision with a second body at rest and continues to move in the original direction but with one fourth of its original speed. What is the mass of the second body?
solution:
From conservation of linear momentum
mU=mU4+MV . . . (i)
For elastic collision: e=1=V-U4u
V=5U4 . . . (ii)
From equation (i) & (ii),
mU=mU4+M5U4
m=m4+54M  ⇒M=3m5=1.2 kg

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A simple pendulum, made of a string of length <math><mi>l</mi></math> and <math><mi>a</mi></math> bob of mass <math><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is released from a small angle <math><msub><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> It strikes a block of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mo>,</mo></math> kept on horizontal surface at its lowest point of oscillations, elastically. It bounces back and goes up to an angle <math><msub><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math><mi>M</mi></math> is given by:
question: A simple pendulum, made of a string of length <math><mi>l</mi></math> and <math><mi>a</mi></math> bob of mass <math><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is released from a small angle <math><msub><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> It strikes a block of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mo>,</mo></math> kept on horizontal surface at its lowest point of oscillations, elastically. It bounces back and goes up to an angle <math><msub><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math><mi>M</mi></math> is given by:
solution:
u=2 gl1-cos θ0 .......(i)

v= velocity of ball after collision

v=m-Mm+Mu

Since ball rises up to angle θ1

v=2gl1-cos θ1=m-Mm+Mu ......(ii)

From (i) and (ii)

m-Mm+M=1-cos θ11-cos θ0=sinθ12sinθ02

⇒Mm=θ0-θ1θ0+θ1⇒M=θ0-θ1θ0+θ1m

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Three blocks A, B and C are lying on a smooth horizontal surface, as shown in the figure. A and B have equal masses, m while C has mass M. Block A is given an initial speed v towards B due to which it collides with B perfectly inelastically. The combined mass collides with C, also perfectly inelastically . 56th of the initial kinetic energy is lost in the whole process. What is the value of M/m?
question: Three blocks A, B and C are lying on a smooth horizontal surface, as shown in the figure. A and B have equal masses, m while C has mass M. Block A is given an initial speed v towards B due to which it collides with B perfectly inelastically. The combined mass collides with C, also perfectly inelastically . 56th of the initial kinetic energy is lost in the whole process. What is the value of M/m?
solution:
mv=m+mv′

m+mv′=m+m+Mvf

∴ final velocity vf=m2m+Mv

Initial energy =12mv2

Final energy  =122m+Mmv2m+M2

Given that 5612mv2=12mv2-122m+Mmv2m+M2

112mv2=12m2v22m+M

6m=2m+M

Mm=4

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q:
Figures (a), (b), (c) and (d) show variation of force with time.

The impulse is highest in figure.
question:
Figures (a), (b), (c) and (d) show variation of force with time.

The impulse is highest in figure.
solution:
As we know, impulse is the area under the curve of the force vs. time graph. So, analysing each figure area under curve.

a A1=12×1.0×0.5=14 N‐s

b A2=0.5×2=1 N‐s

c A3=12×1×0.75=38 N‐s

d A4=12×2×0.5=12 N‐s

From above calculations, area under curve in figure (b) is maximum. Thus, fig (b) impulse is highest.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> collides elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> at rest. After the collision, the two masses move at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with respect to the initial direction of motion of the body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>. The largest possible value of the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> for which the angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be equal, is :
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> moving at speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>V</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> collides elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> at rest. After the collision, the two masses move at angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> with respect to the initial direction of motion of the body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi><mo>.</mo></math>. The largest possible value of the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mi>m</mi></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> for which the angles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>θ</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> will be equal, is :
solution:
Linear momentum conservation
(i) y-direction
mv2sinθ=mv1sinθ
mv2=mv1           ...(1)
(ii) x-direction
mv0=mv2cosθ+mv1cosθ            ...(2)
from (1) and (2)
mv0=mv2cosθ+mv2cosθ
mv0=2mv2cosθ       ...(3)
(iii) e=1given
v2-v1cos2θv0cosθ=1
v2-v1cos2θ=v0cosθ       ...(4)
v2-m22Mcos2θ=v0cosθ
v21-mMcos2θ=v0cosθ
mv02mcosθ1-mMcos2θ=v0cosθ
M-mcos2θ=2mcos2θ
M=2mcos2θ+m2cos2θ-1
Mm=4cos2θ-1
Mmmax=4-1=3

q: The identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>M</mi></math> are placed at the corners of a right angled triangle with mutually perpendicular sides equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> each. Taking point of intersection of these two sides as origin, the magnitude of position vector of the centre of mass of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>, where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________.
question: The identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>M</mi></math> are placed at the corners of a right angled triangle with mutually perpendicular sides equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> each. Taking point of intersection of these two sides as origin, the magnitude of position vector of the centre of mass of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mrow><mi>x</mi></mfrac></math>, where the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ________.
solution:
Position vector r→COM=m1r→1+m2r→2+m3r→3 m1+m2+m3

r→COM =2M×0+2M×4i^+2M×4j^6M

⇒r→=43i^+43j^

|r→|=432+432=423

Therefore, x=3.

q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> collides perfectly elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> at rest. It moves with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> after collision, then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math> is given by
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> collides perfectly elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> at rest. It moves with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> after collision, then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math> is given by
solution:
From momentum conservation

mui^+0=mvj^+3mv→'

v→'=u3i^-v3j^

From kinetic energy conservation 12mu2=12mv2+12(3m)u32+v32

solving v=u2

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Three objects A, B and C are kept in a straight line on a frictionless horizontal surface. The masses of A, B and C are m, 2m and 2m respectively. A moves towards B with a speed of 9 m s-1 and makes an elastic collision with it. Thereafter B makes a completely inelastic collision with C. All motions occur along the same straight line. The final speed of C is :
question: Three objects A, B and C are kept in a straight line on a frictionless horizontal surface. The masses of A, B and C are m, 2m and 2m respectively. A moves towards B with a speed of 9 m s-1 and makes an elastic collision with it. Thereafter B makes a completely inelastic collision with C. All motions occur along the same straight line. The final speed of C is :
solution:
Collision between A and B

m×9=mv1+2 mv2 (from momentum conservation)
e=1=v2-v19
⇒v2=6 m sec-1, v1=-3 m sec-1
Collision between B and C

2 m×6=4mv (from momentum conservation)
v=3 m s-1

q: A rod of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is lying on a horizontal frictionless surface. A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> travelling along the surface hits at one end of the rod with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in a direction perpendicular to the rod. The collision is completely elastic. After collision, particle comes to rest. question: A rod of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math> is lying on a horizontal frictionless surface. A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> travelling along the surface hits at one end of the rod with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> in a direction perpendicular to the rod. The collision is completely elastic. After collision, particle comes to rest. The ratio of masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>m</mi><mi>M</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be
solution:
From momentum conservation, Pi0=Pf
mu=Mv …i
From angular momentum conservation about O,
mu·L2=ML212ω
⇒ω=6muML …(ii)
From e=R.V·SR.V·A
1=V+ωL2u

V+ωL2=u
V+3muM=u
muM+3muM=u
4muM=u
mM=14
x=4

q: Particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> which is at rest initially. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the velocities of particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after collision respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and after collision <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>−</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
question: Particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> collides with another particle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> which is at rest initially. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be the velocities of particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> after collision respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and after collision <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>−</mo><mfenced><mrow><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, the angle between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is :
solution:
m1u→1+m2u→2=m1v→1+m2v→2

2m2(3i^+j^)+m2×0=2m2(i^+3j^)+m2×v→2

23i^+2j^=2i^+23j^+v→2

v→2=(3-1)i^-(3-1)j^

v→1=i^+3j^

Angle between v→1 and v→2 is 105°

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: The center of mass of a thin rectangular plate (fig - x ) with sides of length $a$ and $b$, whose mass per unit area $(\sigma)$ varies as $\sigma=\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}$ (where $\sigma_0$ is a constant), would be $\qquad$
question: The center of mass of a thin rectangular plate (fig - x ) with sides of length $a$ and $b$, whose mass per unit area $(\sigma)$ varies as $\sigma=\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}$ (where $\sigma_0$ is a constant), would be $\qquad$
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & d m=\sigma d A \\ & \begin{aligned} d & =\sigma(d x)(d y)=\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}(d x)(d y) \\ x_{c o m} & =\frac{\int x d m}{\int d m}=\frac{\int x \frac{\left(\sigma_0 x\right)}{a b}(d x)(d y)}{\int_0 \frac{\sigma_0}{a b}(d x)(d y)} \\ & =\frac{\int_0^a x^2 d x \int_0^b d y}{\int_0^b x d x \int_0^b d y}=\frac{2 a}{3} \\ y_{c o m} & =\frac{\int y d m}{\int d m}=\frac{\int y\left(\frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}\right)(d x)(d y)}{\int \frac{\sigma_0 x}{a b}(d x)(d y)}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{\int_0^a x d x \int_0^b y d y}{\int_0^a x d x \int_0^b d y}=\frac{b}{2}$
i.e., $\vec{r}_{\mathrm{com}} \equiv\left(\frac{2 a}{3}, \frac{b}{2}\right)$

q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R). Assertion (A) :
Three identical spheres of same mass undergo one dimensional motion as shown in figure with initial velocities $v_{\mathrm{A}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{C}}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If we wait sufficiently long for elastic collision to happen, then $v_{\mathrm{A}}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, $v_{\mathrm{C}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ will be the final velocities. question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R). Assertion (A) :
Three identical spheres of same mass undergo one dimensional motion as shown in figure with initial velocities $v_{\mathrm{A}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{C}}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If we wait sufficiently long for elastic collision to happen, then $v_{\mathrm{A}}=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_{\mathrm{B}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, $v_{\mathrm{C}}=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ will be the final velocities. Reason (R): In an elastic collision between identical masses, two objects exchange their velocities. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)

q: A uniform thin rod AB of length <math> <mi>L</mi> </math> has linear mass density <math><mi>μ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math> <mi>x</mi> </math> is measured from A. If the CM of the rod lies at a distance of <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> from A, then <math> <mi>a</mi> </math> and <math> <mi>b</mi> </math> are related as:
question: A uniform thin rod AB of length <math> <mi>L</mi> </math> has linear mass density <math><mi>μ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math> <mi>x</mi> </math> is measured from A. If the CM of the rod lies at a distance of <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>L</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> from A, then <math> <mi>a</mi> </math> and <math> <mi>b</mi> </math> are related as:
solution:
Consider an infinitesimal small element of length dx and mars density λ

xcm=∫dmx∫dm=∫λ dxx∫λ dx

=∫0La+bLxxdx∫0La+bLxdx

=∫0Laxdx+∫0LbLx2dx∫0Ladx+∫0LbLxdx

=ax220L+bL x330Lax0L+bLx220L

=a2L2-02+b3L L3-03aL-0+b2LL2-02

=a2L2+bL23aL+bL2=a2L+bL3a+b2

∵xcm=7L12=a2+b3La+b2⇒712= 3a+2b62a+b2

⇒712=3a+2b3×12a+b⇒72a+b=43a+2b

⇒14a+7b=12a+8b⇒2a=b

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Distance of the centre of mass of a solid uniform cone from its vertex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the radius of its base is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and its height is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is equal to:
question: Distance of the centre of mass of a solid uniform cone from its vertex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math>. If the radius of its base is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and its height is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is equal to:
solution:
Let the mass of solid cylinder is M and angle with vertical is θ. Now, we take a small element of thickness dy at a distance y from the vertex. The radius of small element is r, as shown in the figure below.

Then, tanθ=Rh=ry which will give, r=ytanθ. The density of the cone is given by,

ρ=M13πR2h=3MπR2h.

The mass of the small element is given by,

dM=ρdV

dM=3MπR2h×πr2dy=3My2tan2θR2hdy

The center of mass of the cone is given by,

z0=1M∫0hydM

z0=1M∫0hy·3My2tan2θR2hdy

z0=3R2hRh2y440h

z0=3h4.

q: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is dropped from a height <math><mi>h</mi></math> above the ground. At the same time another particle of the same mass is thrown vertically upwards from the ground with a speed of <math><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>g</mi><mi>h</mi></msqrt><mo>.</mo></math> If they collide head-on completely inelastically, the time taken for the combined mass to reach the ground, in units of <math><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></math> is:
question: A particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is dropped from a height <math><mi>h</mi></math> above the ground. At the same time another particle of the same mass is thrown vertically upwards from the ground with a speed of <math><msqrt><mn>2</mn><mi>g</mi><mi>h</mi></msqrt><mo>.</mo></math> If they collide head-on completely inelastically, the time taken for the combined mass to reach the ground, in units of <math><msqrt><mfrac><mrow><mi>h</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>g</mi></mrow></mfrac></msqrt></math> is:
solution:
time for collision t1=h2gh
After t1
VA=0-gt1=-gh2
and VB=2gh-gt1=gh2-12
at the time of collision
p→i=pf→
⇒mV→A+mV→B=2mV→f
⇒-gh2+gh2-12=2V→f
Vf=0

and height from ground

=h-12gt12=h-h4=3h4

so time ,

t=2×3h4g=32 hg

q: If <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>22</mn></mrow></msup></math> gas molecules each of mass <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>26</mn></mrow></msup></math> kg collides with a surface (perpendicular to it) elastically per second over an area <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> with a speed <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo></math> the pressure exerted by the gas molecules will be of the order of:
question: If <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>22</mn></mrow></msup></math> gas molecules each of mass <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>26</mn></mrow></msup></math> kg collides with a surface (perpendicular to it) elastically per second over an area <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> with a speed <math><msup><mrow><mn>10</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>,</mo></math> the pressure exerted by the gas molecules will be of the order of:
solution:
Total momentum transferred in one second =n2mv=10222×10-26×104=2
Force experienced by surface =2 N
Pressure on surface =ForceArea=21=2 Pa

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass 14 kg initially at rest explodes and breaks into three fragments of masses in the ratio $2: 2: 3$. The two pieces of equal masses fly off perpendicular to each other with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ each. The velocity of the heavier fragment is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: A body of mass 14 kg initially at rest explodes and breaks into three fragments of masses in the ratio $2: 2: 3$. The two pieces of equal masses fly off perpendicular to each other with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ each. The velocity of the heavier fragment is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
From mass ratio 2:2:3 with total mass 14 kg: individual masses are 4 kg, 4 kg, and 6 kg.
Setting coordinate axes along the motion directions of the two equal fragments: fragment 1 has momentum $\vec{p_1} = 4 \times 18\hat{i} = 72\hat{i}$ kg⋅m/s, and fragment 2 has $\vec{p_2} = 4 \times 18\hat{j} = 72\hat{j}$ kg⋅m/s.
By momentum conservation, initially at rest: $\vec{p_1} + \vec{p_2} + \vec{p_3} = 0$, so $\vec{p_3} = -72\hat{i} - 72\hat{j}$ kg⋅m/s.
Velocity of 6 kg fragment: $\vec{v_3} = \frac{\vec{p_3}}{6} = -12\hat{i} - 12\hat{j}$ m/s.
Speed: $|\vec{v_3}| = \sqrt{144 + 144} = \sqrt{288} = 12\sqrt{2}$ m/s.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two identical particles move towards each other with velocity $2 v$ and $v$ respectively. The velocity of centre of mass is
question: Two identical particles move towards each other with velocity $2 v$ and $v$ respectively. The velocity of centre of mass is
solution:
Conserving Linear Momentum
$$
2 \mathrm{Mv}_{\mathrm{c}}=2 \mathrm{Mv}-\mathrm{Mv} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{c}}=\mathrm{v} / 2
$$
